text.title: PLG Optimization Tactics Core Rules
text.summary: Six testable rules anchoring PLG optimization. Each pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every tactic in the backlog MUST declare the funnel metric it moves AND a current numeric baseline. Tactics without a baseline are rejected.

rationale.source: Internal PLG audit; cf. Reforge growth-loop teardowns

rule.rationale: Tactics without baselines are theatre — there is no way to detect a lift. The baseline anchors the hypothesis to observable reality.

rule.statement: In-product upgrade prompts MUST trigger at ≤ 80% of the relevant limit (storage, seats, projects). Prompts at 100% are forbidden — the user has already hit the wall.

rationale.source: PostHog + Appcues case studies on upgrade-prompt timing

rule.rationale: Prompts at 100% arrive after the user is blocked and frustrated; 80% gives the user time to act before the workflow breaks.

rule.statement: Zero upgrade prompts during the user's first session. Cap upgrade prompts to one per subsequent session. No prompts during a critical workflow or immediately after a recent dismissal.
rule.rationale: First-session prompts break trust before value is demonstrated; over-prompting trains users to dismiss everything.

rule.statement: All upgrade copy MUST name a comparable customer, quantify the value (hours saved, projects unlocked, revenue captured), and name the specific plan. Two CTAs required: primary (upgrade) + secondary (see example or learn more).

rationale.source: ProfitWell A/B copy tests, Reforge upgrade-copy library

rule.rationale: Named customer + quantified benefit copies convert at 2-3× generic CTA text. Generic copy does not connect the user's current need to the plan's value.

rule.statement: Upgrade CTA text MUST NOT be "Upgrade Now", "Premium Plan", "Get More Features", "Unlock Premium", or any variant. Post-filter the copy generator and reject matches.
rule.rationale: Generic phrases survive review because they sound professional; they do not differentiate plans or signal value.

rule.statement: Free-tier limits MUST give enough surface to reach the Aha moment but include at least one natural upgrade trigger (usage, feature, user, or time limit). Limit changes ship only after finance + retention sign-off.

rationale.source: OpenView PLG report 2024; Stripe SaaS pricing guide

rule.rationale: Too-generous tiers produce low conversion (users never hit limits); too-restrictive produce high abandonment. Limit changes are the highest-leverage decision and need human gating.
